An interview with Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger about Artifact, their new app that is widely available today. Plus, what they learned from Instagram, revisiting the Facebook acquisition, and the future of social media.



View in browser

An Interview with Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger About Artifact

Thursday, February 23, 2023

Listen to this Update in your podcast player

Good morning,

From TechCrunch :

Artifact, the personalized news reader built by Instagram\xe2\x80\x99s co-founders, is now open to the public, no sign-up required. Last month, Instagram\xe2\x80\x99s creators Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger, unveiled their latest venture as an invite-only experience, promising their news app would later evolve to include social elements, like being able to discuss the news with friends. With today\xe2\x80\x99s launch, Artifact is dropping its waitlist and phone number requirements, introducing the app\xe2\x80\x99s first social feature and adding feedback controls to better personalize the news reading experience, among other changes.

I\xe2\x80\x99ve had access to Artifact since the beta launched a few weeks ago , and John Gruber and I discussed our initial impressions on Dithering . We were both a bit underwhelmed, although, as I note in the interview below, I have found the app increasingly useful over the last week in particular. That is on one hand a good thing \xe2\x80\x94 it suggests that Artifact\xe2\x80\x99s core premise, which is that it learns your preferences \xe2\x80\x94 is a valid one; it also raises the question as to just how the new company will get users to stick around long enough to become useful.

I had the chance to discuss this question with Systrom and Krieger, ask what it was like being (very famous) second-time founders, how Artifact fits in the evolution of social media, and what the app might look like in the future. We also touched on Instagram\xe2\x80\x99s beginning, the surprising benefits Systrom and Krieger gained from the acquisition, and the lessons they learned from Facebook that apply to Artifact, and more.

To listen to this interview as a podcast, click the link at the top of this email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.

On to the interview:

An Interview with Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger About Artifact

This interview is lightly edited for clarity.

Topics:
The Second Startup | Artifact | Customer Acquisition and Social Networking | Ranking and Machine Learning | TikTok and the Evolution of Social | Revisiting the Facebook Acquisition | The Future of Artifact and Content

The Second Startup

Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger , welcome to Stratechery.

Kevin Systrom: Thanks for having us.

Mike Krieger: Great to be here.

So this is certainly a sort of founder/startup interview. Indeed, it\xe2\x80\x99s the earliest stage set of co-founders that I\xe2\x80\x99ve ever talked to, so it would seem like an obvious place for my usual opening questions about your background, \xe2\x80\x9cWhat did you do previously?\xe2\x80\x9d, things along those lines. But I think we\xe2\x80\x99re all pretty familiar with your story! I guess that\xe2\x80\x99s why I\xe2\x80\x99m talking to you so early, it sort of makes the point. What\xe2\x80\x99s it been like trying to build something new as the founders of Instagram? You\xe2\x80\x99re not like a random set of co-founders, you are the Instagram founders, how\xe2\x80\x99s that been particularly compared to the first time?

KS: Super easy, right, Mike?

MK: (laughing) Oh yeah. I mean, the things that get easier the second time around, the mistakes that you get to skip and the smoothing function you can apply to the difficulties of the startup journey where somebody once said, \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s never as good as you think it is, it\xe2\x80\x99s never as bad as you think it is\xe2\x80\x9d and you kind of can intellectually believe that the first time around, but you have to have the lived experience of having done it for a few years to really internalize that.

But the hard stuff is still hard. Finding the right product, finding the right partners, finding the right team \xe2\x80\x94 I think it gives you a bit of a leg up, but it\xe2\x80\x99s by no means a sort of cheat code skip to level 10. I think we expected it and we got into it with full eyes open that it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t going to just be you don\xe2\x80\x99t pick up where you left off on Instagram, you\xe2\x80\x99ve learned everything from the first time around but you\xe2\x80\x99ve got to build it back up from scratch.

Is there something to where it\xe2\x80\x99s harder because now you have the pressure because you\xe2\x80\x99re the Instagram guys?

KS: I say to Mike, you can go to all these bookstores or these conferences and get advice about what to do the first time and what not to do the first time, but there\xe2\x80\x99s literally zero books or zero conferences that focus on what not to do the second time and it turns out you just make a whole host of different mistakes. But to look back on our experience, I think that what helps having founded Instagram, is you fully expect the volatility of the experience, it\xe2\x80\x99s not surprising at all. You fully expect to feel elated when something starts working, you fully expect to feel eviscerated whether someone quits or a major customer leaves \xe2\x80\x94 these moments, these crucible moments in your company where you feel like it\xe2\x80\x99s all going to fail. We talk a lot about the second time around we just have this low-pass filter on, it\xe2\x80\x99s all the stuff that it peaks above and goes below, it starts to feel less crazy and it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t make it any easier per se, but you know how to deal with it. That\xe2\x80\x99s been helpful.

Have you had those critical moments already?

KS: Yeah, I mean we started the company, Mike, what, basically two years ago? I mean, in earnest really a year ago, meaning when we finally got a bunch of people and started working on stuff, but I mean machine learning \xe2\x80\x94 I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure how many of your listeners have direct experience with this, but the ones that do I think will commiserate \xe2\x80\x94 machine learning is a little bit like a dark art. It\xe2\x80\x99s one of those things that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work until it works and there\xe2\x80\x99s rarely an in-between. There are some days where we would wake up and just be like, \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know that we can recommend articles to people. It seems like it should work, but I don\xe2\x80\x99t know that this is going to work.\xe2\x80\x9d Then there are other days deep into the experience where we\xe2\x80\x99d wake up and Mike would say, \xe2\x80\x9cI opened up my feed this morning and I\xe2\x80\x99ve never seen such a reflection of me on a screen and this is totally going to work.\xe2\x80\x9d But I mean, I feel like I speak for both Mike and me in saying that it never gets easier the second time around, but you know what to expect.

MK: For sure there\xe2\x80\x99s also an aspect of we\xe2\x80\x99ve done Instagram, we\xe2\x80\x99re coming out with a second thing, that\xe2\x80\x99s a privilege. We\xe2\x80\x99re talking to you and we\xe2\x80\x99ve only been out for three weeks. You\xe2\x80\x99re probably not talking to very-many-if-any three-week-old startups unless they really blow up like IG did the first time around. But for sure, I think we\xe2\x80\x99ve always been our harshest critic, but there\xe2\x80\x99s also this aspect of whatever we launch is going to get viewed and it\xe2\x80\x99s not going to get viewed necessarily as, \xe2\x80\x9cOh cool, the Instagram founders are starting something new and here\xe2\x80\x99s their brand new app, they just launched v1.0.\xe2\x80\x9d, there\xe2\x80\x99s an expectation of polish and craft and all of those things.

Kevin and I had talked, there\xe2\x80\x99s pieces of the app that have felt done for six months that we could have launched six months ago and a combination of our own perfectionism and desire to get it right, but also probably carrying the question of, \xe2\x80\x9cAll right, this is the second thing, we\xe2\x80\x99ve got to get it right from the beginning\xe2\x80\x9d or at least have it feel more baked than we would\xe2\x80\x99ve for an Instagram V1.

Mike, you mentioned the mistakes aspect before. Are there two collections? There are mistakes that you make with every startup and you are making them again, you\xe2\x80\x99re avoiding some, I guess what are some of the ones you avoided is another way to put it, but to go back to your point, Kevin, there\xe2\x80\x99s no book for second-time founders. What are the second-time founder mistakes that are new and original and that you\xe2\x80\x99re getting to experience for the first time?

MK: With Instagram, there was the two of us until we launched and we finally hired a third person that week because we needed somebody to help run community ops and all of that and we hired our first engineer maybe a month after that. That led to a lot of insanity and sleepless nights but we moved quickly and it was the two of us and we could make decisions quickly.

When we started Artifact, we were conscious that we could have started and gone out and hired a team of twenty if we wanted to. There\xe2\x80\x99s at least twenty great people I\xe2\x80\x99ve worked with through the Instagram journey and we\xe2\x80\x99re like, \xe2\x80\x9cNo, we\xe2\x80\x99re not going to go, we\xe2\x80\x99re not going to be that extreme. We\xe2\x80\x99re going to hire fewer than that. Maybe we\xe2\x80\x99ll do seven to ten.\xe2\x80\x9d And honestly, even that was too many for starting it because so much of that early discovery period back when the app is just a sketch and you\xe2\x80\x99re just getting week-to-week you\xe2\x80\x99re sort of finessing from the highest level what the idea is going to be. It\xe2\x80\x99s a bumpy journey and it needs to be, you can\xe2\x80\x99t get too attached to one thing already, I think you\xe2\x80\x99ll funnel down too quickly.

You remembered the pain of only being two people and maybe forgot some of the benefits.

MK: Yeah, and our significant others were like, \xe2\x80\x9cWell you\xe2\x80\x99re definitely not launching this thing, just the two of you guys. We have families now and there\xe2\x80\x99s infrastructure to take care of.\xe2\x80\x9d But I\xe2\x80\x99d say that there\xe2\x80\x99s two categories of mistakes and we\xe2\x80\x99ve learned from them in that as well. One is I think you still need to start small. Seven was too many, maybe it should have been four. The other one is not everybody who is an incredible engineer that does great at an Instagram where the cycles are longer, even if I thought we moved pretty quickly at Instagram, there\xe2\x80\x99s just a different pace, but there\xe2\x80\x99s a lot more certainty on the underlying product. It\xe2\x80\x99s not the shifting sands of a very early stage company. It just takes not necessarily a not necessarily different kind of person, but at least a person ready for a different kind of challenge.

KS: I\xe2\x80\x99d say that the biggest challenge the second time around is not making things perfect before you launch them. Mike, you spoke to this, but I\xe2\x80\x99d say if you ask all of my friends, if you ask my coworkers from Instagram, \xe2\x80\x9cWhat is the number one trait you would describe Kevin as\xe2\x80\x9d? I think perfectionist comes somewhere up in the top three. That doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mean everything that I ever put out is perfect, it just means that I want it to be as perfect as possible.

What we realized with this company in particular is that it\xe2\x80\x99s machine learning, so its main sustenance is data. Unless it has data, it will not work. It can\xe2\x80\x99t exist in a vacuum, it\xe2\x80\x99s not like Instagram where filters work from day one, regardless of the number of users. Our filters this time only work when you have hundreds of thousands of people using it and the machine learning can do its job. We had this moment where we had to decide do we go out with something \xe2\x80\x94 I\xe2\x80\x99d say we\xe2\x80\x99re proud of what we built, but we know it\xe2\x80\x99s not where we want to end. There are a lot of things about it we wish for different. Do you do that given our personalities? That\xe2\x80\x99s a really hard moment. That\xe2\x80\x99s not something that we naturally want to do, but we realize there is no counterfactual here. There\xe2\x80\x99s no world in which in which we stay private with 500 beta testers, most of whom are our closest friends where this thing gets otherworldly good. I mean ChatGPT needs to crawl the entire written Internet to get to where it is today. And even then they complain about running out of text, they want more data.

So the second time around, I think this perfectionism leads us to just be in a very different mode because the first time around we had no idea Instagram was going to be Instagram, we just launched. Sure, we were perfectionists about speed and we wanted to optimize here and there, but literally not a single thought process going on between the two of us, \xe2\x80\x9cWhat are people going to think\xe2\x80\x9d? Because we didn\xe2\x80\x99t care, no one cared. So this time around that was harder but I\xe2\x80\x99d say I think we did the right thing by getting it out. Probably six months too late, but we\xe2\x80\x99re moving now and that\xe2\x80\x99s all that matters. Every single day I wake up, there\xe2\x80\x99s more data and the machine learning gets better and I can see it blossoming and that makes me really excited for the future of what we\xe2\x80\x99re going to build.

Artifact

Well I\xe2\x80\x99m kind of interested in that from a product perspective because I have to say, the more I use Artifact, the more I find it useful. I thought it was not really useful in the first week at all to be honest.

KS: Of course. Yeah, of course.

Over the last couple weeks I\xe2\x80\x99m like, \xe2\x80\x9cWow, I\xe2\x80\x99m actually finding stuff that I wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have found otherwise\xe2\x80\x9d, which obviously is the value proposition. So I have a couple questions on that. Number one, how much of that is the function of learning me versus as you just mentioned it actually is the entire corpus of people using it? So now when there\xe2\x80\x99s thousands or tens of thousands or however many there are using it, what\xe2\x80\x99s the function of improvement there? Is it the mass audience or is it me personally?

KS: I\xe2\x80\x99ll touch on this a little bit. From a machine learning standpoint, the idea of cold start is as old as the domain of machine learning. The idea is, okay, you have someone who shows up, how good can you be and how quickly can you be good for them? The problem is, Ben, when you show up, you select a handful of interests. I\xe2\x80\x99m not sure what you selected, but you selected some of them. We probably know you\xe2\x80\x99re in a specific location, meaning general locale, we don\xe2\x80\x99t know exactly where you are, but general locale. So the question really is how quickly can you go from basically blank slate default, which you said wasn\xe2\x80\x99t useful, and I\xe2\x80\x99d agree in general, to more and more useful? Mike has been using this product for, I don\xe2\x80\x99t know, a year and a half, maybe longer, and we literally know every nuance of what Mike\xe2\x80\x99s into. Mike really loves F1 car racing and he\xe2\x80\x99s super into it.

Oh me too! That\xe2\x80\x99s where I found the most links that I\xe2\x80\x99ve shared.

KS: It will figure out the drivers he cares about, the teams he cares about, the drama he cares about and it will reliably serve him that. For me, it knows I love Japanese architecture, which is a very weird thing. Meaning it\xe2\x80\x99s not weird, but it\xe2\x80\x99s a very specific interest, it\xe2\x80\x99s not like you\xe2\x80\x99re going to sign up for something and tap Japanese architecture is your interest. That takes probably on the order of 100 to 1,000 reads, meaning taps into articles to start to learn that level of nuance. The interesting part here is that with lots of data, we can group you into an archetype of a person pretty quickly because it turns out, and I was telling Mike this the other day, the most fascinating thing about running this company so far and really working on the data personally, is that people like to think they\xe2\x80\x99re very unique people with very unique consumption experience or tastes, and it turns out there are probably literally thousands of Ben\xe2\x80\x99s out there and I speak in general.

It\xe2\x80\x99s the Mike bucket, we just established that. I\xe2\x80\x99m in the Mike Krieger bucket, just drop Ben Thompson in there.

KS: I\xe2\x80\x99m speaking in very general terms, but in general there are archetypes of people and the thing is if you can pinpoint what archetype Ben is quickly enough, you can actually start to serve them things that feel personalized even more quickly. And they say, \xe2\x80\x9cWait, how do you know that\xe2\x80\x9d? It turns out, well, we just know if you like interior design, you probably like architecture and you probably like recipes and cooking because we have lots of people that are exactly like that. If we\xe2\x80\x99re wrong you probably write it off because you\xe2\x80\x99re like, \xe2\x80\x9cWhatever, they\xe2\x80\x99re serving me a recipe, who cares?\xe2\x80\x9d and we figure out from that mistake pretty quickly. So the answer \xe2\x80\x94 just to be very, very direct \xe2\x80\x94 is it\xe2\x80\x99s both. It\xe2\x80\x99s more data from you, but I bet you we have almost no data on you so far and then also it means lots of people signing up so that we can kind of understand these vague notions of a type of person so that we can serve more targeted things more quickly. This is all stuff that\xe2\x80\x99s been used.

It feels like a sculpture approach. Instead of forming a persona, you quickly sort them into a persona and then chip away the parts that actually aren\xe2\x80\x99t right and that\xe2\x80\x99s sort of a shortcut to get there more quickly.

KS: Well said, and I\xe2\x80\x99m probably going to steal that from now on, I appreciate it.

Call yourself Michelangelo!

KS: (laughing) It\xe2\x80\x99s good.

Customer Acquisition and Social Networking

How much of a challenge do you feel in this \xe2\x80\x9cGetting to usefulness\xe2\x80\x9d quickly? I\xe2\x80\x99m sure you had a whole bunch of signups particularly at the beginning and it\xe2\x80\x99s very easy to churn out very, very quickly, particularly if that experience is not great. I know you\xe2\x80\x99ve done stuff like streaks \xe2\x80\x94 which it\xe2\x80\x99s hilarious how good this gamification works because it\xe2\x80\x99s the stupidest idea ever that I have a streak of reading news articles, and yet I keep clicking that stupid notification to keep my streak alive. Has that been a challenge that you\xe2\x80\x99ve seen to date is just people try it out, then they churn out super quickly, how do you get them coming back?

MK: One thing that\xe2\x80\x99s been interesting, and this is as a second-time founder and having gotten to see Facebook that does user growth and analytics, I think better than anybody, we got to see it firsthand and apply the best of that to Instagram, is trying to calibrate what of that should you bring to bear when you have a beta group of 500 to 1,000 people? Are you just kidding yourself that you\xe2\x80\x99re not going to get statistical significant data at all and what conceptually still matters? So even though we were onboarding, what was it like fifty to one hundred people for a while just through TestFlight, pre-launch-

KS: Per week.

MK: We were still being pretty analytical around what are their cohort curves look like? For the people that sign up do they stick around? What brings them back? What\xe2\x80\x99s their dropoff point? Is there a clearing point where you\xe2\x80\x99ve read enough that it knows you? And a couple things that stand out that I think kind of speak to this sort of \xe2\x80\x9cHow good do we have to be on the first one\xe2\x80\x9d? Obviously we\xe2\x80\x99d like to be as good as possible and the more data we get the better we can get on that first run, but assume we\xe2\x80\x99re not going to be perfect because we\xe2\x80\x99re not.

I think a couple things that really help \xe2\x80\x94 one is that Artifact isn\xe2\x80\x99t a product where if your friends aren\xe2\x80\x99t on it\xe2\x80\x99s just not interesting and if they never join, it\xe2\x80\x99s never going to be interesting. There\xe2\x80\x99s a lot of great content out there that\xe2\x80\x99s getting produced every day and we find people that even who might kick the tires, site it\xe2\x80\x99s not working for them yet, do come back later. Sometimes it\xe2\x80\x99s a week, some days it\xe2\x80\x99s months. Sometimes we\xe2\x80\x99re like, \xe2\x80\x9cWow, this person showed back up.\xe2\x80\x9d It\xe2\x80\x99s not like we learned anything more about them in the meantime, but we learned enough about people like them that the product got better or we added some new sources that were useful.

Two is push notifications, which we try to be thoughtful and smart about what we actually send to people, but that\xe2\x80\x99s a great other way in which people like maybe a week later like, \xe2\x80\x9cOh, that actually is a really good story that I want to click on\xe2\x80\x9d, and \xe2\x80\x9cHey, the feed got better for these different reasons\xe2\x80\x9d. So hopefully we get people in and there\xe2\x80\x99s always a group that\xe2\x80\x99s going to stick. Then what\xe2\x80\x99s kind of fun about Artifact that\xe2\x80\x99s a bit different than Instagram is we got these other touch points over time that might be a trigger point for somebody checking it out again. Maybe we have some new announcement where they\xe2\x80\x99ll bring it back in and I think as long as people give us enough tries over time I\xe2\x80\x99m confident we keep getting better.

What are your social plans? Casey Newton mentioned in your launch interview that there would be the ability to comment on links and have direct messages conversations. As far as I can tell, it hasn\xe2\x80\x99t been turned on broadly to date. There\xe2\x80\x99s obviously an Instagram connection here: Chris Dixon wrote that famous post about Instagram, \xe2\x80\x9cCome for the tools, stay for the social network.\xe2\x80\x9d Is there a similar strategy here? It certainly seems like this could be a backdoor attempt to take on Twitter, which is a fairly insane goal for lots of reasons. Or is this all about algorithm and recommendations with a little bit of conversation tacked on? Obviously, you talked about the cold start problem with social networks, this is a great way to have content from day one, but is this the end state or does this evolve into a social network?

KS: Mike likes to give me crap sometimes because I spend a lot of time talking about what we\xe2\x80\x99re going to be like five years from now, and I think it\xe2\x80\x99s important to kind of draw the line between what we are now and as second-time founders understanding that you always start with a kernel, and then you let that thing blossom over time, but that we\xe2\x80\x99re just kind of crazy enough to think that we will be around five years from now, so we can have a five-year plan.

Where we started is really, it\xe2\x80\x99s the beachhead. It\xe2\x80\x99s what can we be good at enough, with enough people and provide unique value that they\xe2\x80\x99ll sign up and they\xe2\x80\x99ll use it, and we\xe2\x80\x99ll retain some portion of them? Mike was talking about retention curves. What\xe2\x80\x99s crazy about the scientific method is you do all these experiments at a small scale and then you have tons of people sign up, and the curves look identical and you go, \xe2\x80\x9cOkay, well we did all these experiments when we were small, and now it scales to when it\xe2\x80\x99s larger and now we keep these people and they start finding value.\xe2\x80\x9d Then they hang out and they start to use it and their data makes their feeds better, it makes other people\xe2\x80\x99s feeds better. Then you can say to them, \xe2\x80\x9cHey, wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t it be great if X,\xe2\x80\x9d X being this additional utility that makes the experience even better, but it would\xe2\x80\x99ve felt completely empty and suboptimal if you had had it from day one.

I think the mistake too many entrepreneurs make is they start companies assuming they have scale. We\xe2\x80\x99ve seen no scale, we\xe2\x80\x99ve seen hyperscale, and what we realized was what we have to do is we have to start with that utility and the best networks. I didn\xe2\x80\x99t realize Chris wrote that post, but I should maybe in the back of my mind that\xe2\x80\x99s what I was quoting and maybe I had read it, but I think all social networks start off as a utility and blossom into social networks long term, and that\xe2\x80\x99s because they earn the right to be a social network, not because they start as a social network. That\xe2\x80\x99s kind of where I think we are, is this utility phase where we\xe2\x80\x99re trying to be as good as possible for our beginning users and we\xe2\x80\x99re going to see as we grow what we\xe2\x80\x99re allowed to add based on how many users we get.

Yeah, that certainly makes sense. One thing as far as the main social feature that I\xe2\x80\x99ve encountered that I thought was interesting, and it\xe2\x80\x99s not really a social feature, but is when you share a link to Artifact, and I swear I almost had a stroke when this happened and someone clicks that link\xe2\x80\x94

KS: This is Mike\xe2\x80\x99s feature, I\xe2\x80\x99m going to blame Mike.

You get a notification that someone opened your link and the first time I was horrified, I\xe2\x80\x99m like, \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t want that to happen\xe2\x80\x9d and I quickly realized that, no, actually I love it, that\xe2\x80\x99s amazing. I want to know if people click my link and it was like I was just observing myself with a very clear demonstration of a revealed preference versus stated preference. If you had asked me if I wanted anyone to know that when someone clicked a link, I would say, \xe2\x80\x9cNo way. That\xe2\x80\x99s creepy, that\xe2\x80\x99s weird.\xe2\x80\x9d Turns out I actually really like it.

Ranking and Machine Learning

And I\xe2\x80\x99m interested in this in the context of, you go back to Instagram and Kevin, you mentioned in your interview with Casey that one of the lessons you learned was the way people responded to the algorithmic timeline. You go back when that was introduced, I was super pro and I\xe2\x80\x99m like, \xe2\x80\x9cOf course people like it better\xe2\x80\x9d, but there was a lot of outrage at the time. I\xe2\x80\x99m curious \xe2\x80\x94 were you in favor of that from when it actually launched? Both of you can weigh in on this, was there a lot of trepidation in Instagram? \xe2\x80\x9cLook, we\xe2\x80\x99ve always been a certain way, if you ask anyone they says they want want it the same way, but we\xe2\x80\x99re going to actually make this change and oh it turns out it\xe2\x80\x99s better\xe2\x80\x9d, how has your thinking shifted as far as you have to give people what they don\xe2\x80\x99t know they want, even if it seems a little weird or discomforting at first?

KS: Do you remember we actually added machine learning and ranking not to feed first, but where do we add it? We added it to the Explore page, right?

MK: Explore, yeah.
